title: Filling metric spaces
speaker: Alexander Nabutovsky
affiliation: University of Toronto; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Abstract: Uryson k-width of a metric space X measures how close X is to being k-dimensional. Several years ago Larry Guth proved that if M is a closed n-dimensional manifold, and the volume of each ball of radius 1 in M does not exceed a certain smallconstant e(n), then the Uryson (n-1)-width of M is less than 1. This result is a significant generalization of the famous Gromov's inequality relating the volume and the filling radius that plays a central role in systolic geometry.
tags: Workshop on Geometric Functionals: Analysis and Applications

proof_stages.label: Classical filling-radius framework and systolic obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Gromov's filling-radius inequality for the Kuratowski embedding and explains, via an impossible-extension argument for essential manifolds, why filling radius controls the shortest noncontractible closed geodesic.

proof_stages.label: Guth's local-volume Uryson-width problem
proof_stages.summary: Guth's upgrades replace global volume by small volumes of unit balls and strengthen filling-radius control to small Uryson width, leading to the question of whether the same statement holds for arbitrary compact metric spaces using Hausdorff content.

proof_stages.label: Hausdorff-content theorem and consequences
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem verifies Guth's content conjecture: if every radius-r ball has sufficiently small m-dimensional Hausdorff content, then the (m-1)-dimensional Uryson width is at most r, with corollaries for content filling inequalities, collapse criteria, and quantitative Szpilrajn-type dimension bounds.

proof_stages.label: Gromov-Wenger inductive filling mechanism
proof_stages.summary: A proof model for filling inequalities is reviewed: cone/coarea inequalities and induction cut off thin fingers along small slices, fill the cuts in one lower dimension, reduce volume by a fixed factor, and iterate until coning off the remainder.

proof_stages.label: Nerve maps and skeleton-pushing in Guth's proof
proof_stages.summary: Guth's proof covers the manifold by small balls, maps it by Lipschitz coordinate functions to a rectangular nerve, and deforms the map face-by-face to the (n-1)-skeleton so that fibers have small diameter.

proof_stages.label: Exponential good-ball estimates
proof_stages.summary: To control Lipschitz losses from repeated projections in high-dimensional nerves, the cover must have good balls whose radii and volumes/contents decay exponentially with overlap multiplicity; the later radius-selection algorithm obtains this from sub-Euclidean growth.

proof_stages.label: Nonadditivity obstruction for Hausdorff content
proof_stages.summary: Replacing measure by Hausdorff content preserves monotonicity, subadditivity, and Lipschitz control, but the absence of additivity for disjoint sets breaks both the cut-and-fill reduction and Guth's exponential-estimate arguments.

proof_stages.label: Content-level substitutes for measure arguments
proof_stages.summary: The proof repairs the measure arguments by enforcing small optimal covering radii to recover enough quasi-additivity on separated balls and by proving the needed coarea/Kary slicing inequality for Hausdorff content.

examples.note: These familiar families make the abstract essential-manifold hypothesis concrete before the filling-radius-to-systole argument.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker not only lists these as essential manifolds but then carries out the extension/retraction proof in the aspherical case, mapping vertices, edges, and extending over skeleta

examples.name: Unit ball with very large negative curvature
examples.note: The ball has huge Riemannian volume but small Hausdorff content because it is covered by one radius-one ball, and its later return illustrates why cutting a small hole need not reduce content.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the negatively curved unit ball by comparing its huge volume with a one-ball Hausdorff-content cover and later reasons what happens after cutting a small hole

passes.reason: The highly regular constant-negative-curvature ball illustrates that Hausdorff content can be far smaller than volume or measure.

examples.name: Euclidean disk with one-dimensional Hausdorff content
examples.note: Since HC_1 of the disk behaves like diameter, removing half the disk or adding tiny blobs shows the failure of additivity and forces a change from volume-reduction arguments.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the one-dimensional Hausdorff content of the Euclidean disk via diameter/covering reasoning and then uses cuts and blobs to illustrate nonadditivity

examples.name: Two comparable disjoint balls and their optimal content covers
examples.note: This toy configuration identifies overlap between content-optimal covers as the mechanism of nonadditivity and motivates requiring contents so small that only tiny covering balls are useful.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through two comparable disjoint balls by analyzing optimal covers, overlap, small versus big covering balls, and a lower bound for the content of their union
